comments.title: Sphere of Protection in the Grove

Yanocoches

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection in the Grove
replies.content:
There's a lot of flexibility in the Sphere of Protection. When I was doing it regularly in solitary Grove workings, I started by going to the north side of the altar, facing south, and doing the Elemental Cross there; then I went to the east, and faced outward there and in the south, west, and north to invoke and banish the elements. Then I returned to the north of the altar, facing south, to invoke Spirit and do the three circulations of the light.

As for crankiness, good heavens, an occasional grumpy mood is nothing to worry about.

replies.title: Re: Druids at Pantheacon
replies.content: It's been a very long time since I've attended Pantheacon and so I have no idea whether you can find a congenial group of Druids there. I didn't see a lot of woowoo, furries, or cannabis, though there was a lot of Pagan politics the last few times I went.  On the other hand, the divination panels used to be pretty good, and Lon is always worth listening to, not to mention funnier than anything.

replies.title: Re: Druids at Pantheacon
replies.content:
I attend Pantheacon almost every year. Glenn Turner puts a complete program schedule up on the Pantheacon website about a month before it happens (which is always the Presidents Day weekend in February, 2/15-2/18 2019). This is helpful to plan ahead which programs you want to go to and which to skip. There are multiple tracks of activities going on from morning until almost midnight on Saturday and Sunday.

In addition to the regular con programming, there are a couple of dozen hospitality suites hosted by organizations, which have their own specialized events and get-togethers. In 2017, three different Druid organizations had separate suites. Some years Druid suites are cohosted by more than one group.

There are also scheduled book signings by authors, frequently ones who are presenting at the con. If there is someone you particularly want to talk to, that would be an opportunity to meet them.

I'm sure there are furries at Pantheacon but they keep a low profile. I don't recall any open furry suites. Some people walk around in spectacular costumes, but I don't remember any full coverage animal suits.

Some types of lectures and workshops recur from year to year, but other themes crop up in streaks that last a couple of years and then recede. I remember years when there was an abundance of rituals and talks about various Afro-Diasporic religions, about various minority sexual orientations, about political activism, etc. I don't want to minimize any of these themes as fads, but they come and go, and if you only attend Pcon once in awhile, you might get a misleading picture of the interests of the wider community from whatever stuff the schedulers decide to give a lot of program slots to that year.

There will absolutely be an overload of woowoo if you are indiscriminate, but depending on your interests, it's possible to spend the entire weekend going just to scholarly presentations or crafts and live music or whatever interests you, plus shopping and grabbing a meal and talking to people. It is easy to become overstimulated by the crowds and the nonstop activity. If you are planning to stay more than one night, which you probably will do if you are coming from a distance, allow for some quiet time with people you already know or alone and recognize that you can't go to everything that interests you. If your schedule permits, try to arrive by Friday morning before the full crowd does, so you can get settled in your hotel (if you are staying in one) and get your bearings.

comments.title: Jesus vs Hu

replies.title: Re: Jesus vs Hu
replies.content:
Over the three centuries since the Druid Revival got going, a solid majority of the Druids in that tradition have invoked Jesus as well as the various Druid gods. It's not at all uncommon in the tradition, in fact, to identify Hu the Mighty with God the Father, and Hesus the chief of tree-spirits with Jesus the son of God. So in working with both Christian and Druid deities, you're actually in the mainstream of the tradition, and I'm kind of over to one side! ;-)

The broader point worth making here, of course, is that what gods you worship is up to you and Them. If other people get their knickers in a knot over that, that's their problem, not yours.

replies.title: Re: Jesus vs Hu

Baptism is an initiation; like any initiation, it brings you into contact with the egregor (that is, the collective consciousness) of the tradition in which you're being initiated, and like any of the deeper initiations, it brings you into contact with a current of consciousness and energy from levels well above the human -- in this case, as in many others, from the god who established the tradition and its lineage.

It's a serious matter. If you've never been baptized, you'll want to discuss it with the gods you work with, meditate on it, and do divination, until you're comfortable with whatever choice you decide to make.

replies.title: Re: Order of things
replies.content:
You don't do the Middle Pillar in an open temple. What most students of the tradition do, and what I do, is to practice the banishing LRP, the exercise of the Central Ray (our version of the Middle Pillar), and meditation, in that order, every day. comments.title: Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram
comments.content:
Hello --

I have recently begun working through the Celtic Golden Dawn book and have a question regarding the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram. It begins by instructing the practitioner to do the invoking ritual then later teaching the banishing and says to go back and forth between the two, alternating which ritual is done before the meditation of the day.

I've read elsewhere to perform the ritual up to three times a day. If one were to do so, would you suggest alternating between invoking and banishing or stick to one or the other? At this point, I still don't really understand what is being performed by this act and would love some clarification.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram
replies.content:
Okay, what you read elsewhere isn't necessarily applicable to the training program given in The Celtic Golden Dawn! As a rule, when you're doing a system of training out of a given book or correspondence course, go with what the book or course says, and leave what you read elsewhere aside for the time being.

In the case of The Celtic Golden Dawn, the point of the training is to get used to working with, and perceiving, nonphysical energies. Doing the LRP before your daily meditation every day, and alternating summoning and banishing versions of the ritual, is a good way to develop the skills and sensitivities you need. Doing it much more often can be a bad idea -- you can overload yourself, and burn out. Take it a step at a time and you'll get further; put the energy you'd put into more LRPs into mastering the rest of the Ovate curriculum, and you'll get much further.

replies.content:

comments.title: Questions
comments.content:
Good day to you Sir,

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
1) I use homeopathic medicines -- specifically, the biochemic cell salts, which have been the mainstay of my household health care for the last thirty years -- to deal with such things. Herbs and acupressure also seem to work well; I don't know of a magical method that's anything like as effective.

2) If you have your friend's permission to do so, prayer is always a useful option in such situations.

3) Yes, but I had the great good fortune of watching enough people crash and burn trying to get rich through magic to figure out what not to do, and the Order of Essenes correspondence course I discussed over in my other blog helped me figure out what actually works. The very short form is that changing yourself is always more effective than changing the world around you; if you work magic to develop your talents and skills, to shed bad habits that keep you from making the income you need, to assist you in finding and pursuing opportunities, and -- this especially -- to learn to waste less of the money you have, you'll prosper. The fascinating thing is that as you change yourself, the world changes with you, while if you try to change the world without changing yourself, the world resists you at every turn.

replies.title: seasonal allergies
replies.content:
I used to have severe allergic reactions to the pollen that privet trees release in the summer. On the advice of my herbalist, I started taking two capsules daily of either dried nettles or a proprietary compound called HistaEze that contains nettle extract, another plant and some minerals, starting one month before the time that I usually start having seasonal allergies, and continuing through the period of the allergies. it is important to start ingesting the dried nettles weeks before you start having allergic symptoms, because the effect is not quick-acting.

This has helped me tremendously. I no longer have to take massive daily doses of OTC antihistamines and oral Prednisone during the heaviest pollen releases, just one antihistamine pill a couple of times a week and I'm fine.

Dried nettle capsules are sold in drugstores in the vitamin section and are pretty cheap. The proprietary supplement I mentioned is more expensive. My herbalist says that nettles are chock full of helpful minerals and that fresh young nettles (cooked) are a good spring tonic. You can consume nettles year round and they don't dry out your mucous membranes.

replies.title: Re: magic involved?
replies.content: Good question. It might be coincidences, it might be something more. While you've said you don't intend to start practicing magic, but I'd encourage you to consider doing a standard hoodoo bath to remove negative influences. In case you don't recall the method, it's simple enough; fill a tub partway with water; dissolve in it a quarter cup of Kosher salt, a quarter cup of vinegar and a pinch of cayenne pepper. Stand in the tub and, with a clean cloth, wash yourself in the water starting from your head and always moving the cloth downward. When you're finished, get dressed, take some of the water (the rest can be allowed to drain out), and throw it on the roots and trunk of the biggest and healthiest tree you can conveniently find. Do this three times on three successive days, and see what happens; if it's hostile magic, you should notice a sharp improvement.

replies.title: Re: magic involved?
replies.content:

2) If you only put a quarter cup of vinegar in a good quantity of water, you won't smell of vinegar. On the other hand, if you want to shower afterwards, you can do so as soon as you dump the water on the tree.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Since you've already completed the Dolmen Arch course -- and in exemplary form, may I add -- by all means experiment with adding different elements from compatible magical systems. Butler's work ought to fit quite well, since his books were among the earliest texts I used in my own magical training.

2) There are many different ways to do the reverse recollection; it's one of the oldest practices in Western occultism -- it was in use in the Pythagorean brotherhood in 500 BC -- so variations are unavoidable. I find that most people do best with the version I present, but your mileage may vary; try the other and see how it works for you.

3) The reason's actually quite simple. The traditions of natural magic we've inherited were codified before Uranus and Neptune were discovered, and it's only very recently that enough people have been interested in magic that it was possible to get past simply preserving and teaching the old lore, and start building on it again. It'll take a couple of centuries of experiment, observation, and testing to work out exactly what herbs, stones, etc. correspond to Uranus and Neptune.

comments.title: feng shui magic
comments.content:
Just copying and pasting my question from last week at Ecosophia into its proper place!

John Michael,

My wife is fairly practiced in the art of feng shui, and since I’ve become less materialist and more receptive to such things, she insists that it’s time to do something about where I sleep in the house.

Despite a constant and protracted effort toward eating better, herbal supplementation, etc, my physical health has been steadily deteriorating for the past…almost 5 years, which is precisely when we moved our bed from a location that was just “not-optimal” for me according to my pa-kua lo shu numerology to a place where it was downright dismal. A year later we built a privacy wall between us and the stairs because our kids (upstairs) were getting old enough to…need it.

Things got noticeably worse for my situation immediately. I assume the wall made the energy flow even worse than it was before. I just didn’t believe in such “rubbish” at the time, so I didn’t think of that as a potential solution. And so the suffering continued.

Again over the past couple of days my health has taken another hard knock, and over tea this morning (after rereading your new post to her obviously) we decided it was time to move the bed. In our little cabin the options are pretty limited, but we were able to turn the bed 90* from West to North, keeping my wife in a #2 auspicious position but moving me from a #8 inauspicious to a #3 auspicious position.

But here’s the rub. I haven’t even slept in the bed yet and I’m already getting relief from my increasingly unbearable arthritis issues. The wrench in my back that developed yesterday for no real reason and was keeping me from standing, sitting, or even breathing normally, is dissipating steadily, and the chronic arthritis in my right hip is nowhere to be found, after only a few hours!!

How is this possible?? I thought I’d at least have to sleep in the bed one measly night to start getting relief. But no, I’m getting it from just the act of moving my sleeping spot from a terrible to an auspicious position! I mean, yee-ha!, but wha?

...the benefit of a few days of sitting on this question is that I have additional related news. My general vigor seems to be slowly and steadily returning, I'm sleeping more consistently and going back to sleep faster when disturbed, and my wife says I'm snoring about 90% less than before.

Tripp

I've been practicing the 3 Rays rite and Central Ray, and getting to know the Gods of Druidry and the spirits and beings in my own yard and neighborhood through the seasons. Afterwards I wondered why- if I were at home and not in a theater, that wide-open black box of imaginal space, and physically closer to the land spirits and locale of current practice would I have called on them instead? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As Manly P. Hall liked to say, "The best place is where you are, and now is the appropriate time."

An exercise that worked very well for me is to sit down for fifteen minutes early in the day and write. The rule is that your pen, or your fingers on the keyboard, or whatever you're using must not stop moving for the entire fifteen minutes. You don't have to write about anything in particular; if all you can think of to write is "I don't understand the point of this exercise, I have no idea what to write about," and so on, then write that. Set a timer, and keep writing until the fifteen minutes are up.

The goal of this exercise is to break through the mental barriers that keep you from writing. Do it every day, and watch what happens!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Mr. Greer-
Thanks for answering everyone's questions!

What is your opinion of the theory of humorism? Namely, the idea that people are divided into one of four personality groups, based upon the presence or absence of certain bodily fluids?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, classic humoral psychology is a little more subtle than that. There are, according to Galen, Avicenna, and the other writers in the tradition, four basic elemental types, but every human being has all four humors, and next to nobody is a perfect fit for any one type. For example, I've got a generally melancholic temperament but tilted a little toward the choleric side, and fairly close to the midpoint of balance; there are plenty of melancholic people who are a lot more melancholic than I am, and some of them tend toward the phlegmatic rather than the choleric side of the temperament!

As a system, it's highly useful, not least because it also has healing applications -- herbs and other healing substances also have their humoral balance, and can be used to repair yours when you're thrown off balance by an illness -- and it's the basis for the Western equivalent of traditional Chinese medicine or Ayurveda. It would be good to see some serious efforts made to revive it in the years ahead.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Isn’t the theory of humours somewhat medically discredited, at least for medicine? I heard it’s ok for psychology.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Discredited" is a slippery word. These days, out of the mouth of an MD, it pretty reliably means "this doesn't yield profits for the medical and pharmaceutical industries."

The humoral theory doesn't work well in a literal sense -- that is, there doesn't seem to be any correlation between health conditions and the relative concentration of blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile in the body. Treat the humors as energetic states, like yin and yang in Chinese medicine, and they work very well as a way of tracking whole-system shifts in health -- the kind of thing that today's medical industry is by and large incompetent at dealing with.

comments.title: No Vacancy?
comments.content:
You've written at some length in recent months about reincarnation and the journey of souls who have taken on human bodies. In another decade or two there will be eight billion of us knocking about.

You've also described how, in our several centuries long descent into a new Dark Age, the human population is likely to be drastically reduced...possibly to as low as 500 million to one billion. replies.title: Re: No Vacancy?
replies.content:
It's not capricious at all. Here's the way I think of it.

Until about five hundred years ago, there had always been fewer than 500 million human beings on earth. (Current estimates insist that this number has always been creeping slowly upwards, but I strongly suspect that this is influenced by the myth of progress, and that global population had been cycling between 250 million and 500 million for some millennia before then.) In that year there were between 30 and 60 million buffalo on the North American plains; the total number of large relatively intelligent mammals in the world was orders of magnitude greater than the human population. Now that has reversed; there are getting on for eight billion humans on the planet, and the total number of large intelligent mammals is much smaller.

In Druid teachings, animals also have souls, and each human soul has reached the human level after countless lifetimes in a vast number of different animal forms. Each human soul then goes through a series of human lives, developing the distinctive capacities that come with being fully sentient, before going on to suprahuman levels of being. What happened to all those countless millions of animal souls as the human population exploded and animal populations crashed to a tiny fraction of their current level?

The animals were reborn as human beings, of course.

Compare the collective intelligence of people nowadays with that of people from earlier centuries. One of my favorite touchstones is the Lincoln-Douglas debates from the 1856 election -- a set of debates held before ordinary working class voters in an Illinois Congressional election. Compare them to current presidential elections, and it's like comparing a book by physicist Richard Feynman to a book by Dr. Seuss. Something has caused the average capacity for information processing and abstract thought to crash over the last century and a half.

I suggest that what's happened is that a lot of animal souls that were not yet ready to move on into human incarnation ended up becoming incarnated as human beings anyway, because that's the only kind of body that was readily available. Once the human population crashes again and animal populations expand, most of them will go back to animal form, and there will be a lot of very clever animals for a while! Then, in due time, they'll finish their work in animal forms, incarnate either as human beings or as whatever intelligent species replaces us, and go on.

comments.title: Acceptance of suffering

JMG,

I suppose the most overt example would be Christianity and the holy obligation to figuratively imitate Christ in his acceptance of crucifixion - I believe in the Church there's even the notion of the "victim soul", the rare soul who, like Christ, elects to, and receives, Divine permission to, take on the sufferings of the world. comments.title: When is bad luck a test, when is it warning or a curse, and when is it just chance?
comments.content:
Hi John,

In the last few months all manner of improbable, chaotic challenges have arisen all around me, one after another. If it was one thing or two I wouldn't be bringing it up, but (1) it is one thing after another after another - when even any single event would normally be the worst thing in of a year. And (2) all this seemed to start as soon as I made a personal vow to - I thought - begin really focusing on my mission in life. Things started off "lucky" and then there has been one unlikely, "unlucky" set-back after another.

I am torn between (a) I am being tested for resolve (b) I am being cursed (c) I am being discouraged by fate (d) I am being silly -- trying to read too much into a string of "bad luck".

There are doubtless a few people who aren't particularly happy I've made certain decisions. Or maybe I'm just due some karma (although I'm not clear for what as I tend to be very considerate of others). Or well ... I'm not sure how to interpret all this stuff that started so suddenly and just keeps on coming....

It's not much in my nature to quit, so I am mostly trying to grin and bear it, but the "series of unfortunate events" just seem to go on and on ... one after another rather than all at once. So I would kind of like the fates to get to the point or have the storm winds die down or something for a while.

What is a good luck charm in such a case? Or would not even advise that?

How and when do you decide you are being warned by "fate" vs being tested for resolve? Or do you even believe the universe tests you when you make a major resolution and set out to change your life dramatically? And when do you try not to think too much about the string of bad luck? I've been trying to consult the IChing and it is sometimes interesting but just vague enough I'm not finding it too helpful.

I began putting a saucer of vinegar next to my bed a few days ago just in case. And I took notes of today's suggestion on how to take a bath to cleanse the etheric body. Is that the best place to start?

replies.title: Re: When is bad luck a test, when is it warning or a curse, and when is it just chance?

In effect, you need to clear the decks of karmic burdens that will otherwise get in the way of your work, and that usually involves plowing face first into a bunch of ugly situations. Keep your chin up, deal with the crises as they come up, and continue to pursue your work; after a while -- which will vary depending on how much karmic crud you have to clear away -- it'll be over, and things will start going very much better than they've gone before.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not possession; it's a very straightforward bit of psychology. Your ego, the thing you think of as "yourself," is simply a mental image of who you are, held in your own mind; if you change that image, you change how you behave. Taking on a "Scout Name" or some other borrowed identity is an effective tool when you need to change how you relate to the world.

When an identity has been used over and over again by many people for many years -- and I still remember when I was a Cub Scout, and grinned from ear to ear as I recognized the names from what was then one of my favorite books -- you don't have to know about the identity consciously for it to affect you, you just have to use the name.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'd like to echo what our host said here.

For whatever it may be worth, growing up back in the '40s, I picked up the notion (probably from my mother's family, my ancestral line with esoteric interests) that every healthy person naturally has a multiplicity of distinct Selves within a single body, each with its own strengths and weaknesses, and that you should shift from one Self to another in the course of each day as your circumstances change.

These different Selves, we thought, naturally develop as one grows up: a newborn has little or no Self at all, but creates many Selves for itself as it deals with the great challenges of growing from a helpless infant into a fully competent adult. Much of this process happens outside of the infant's or child's conscious awareness, so adults usually have no natural awareness of their various Selves. This is something people have to work at: it is hard work, but well worth the effort

(Rarely, as a pathology, the Selves remain ignorant of one another, and may even adopt different personal names; then you get what used to be called "multiple personality disorder." That's not what we're talking about here.)

Part of becoming an adult is getting to know many --ideally, all--your individual Selves and mastering the process of shifting back and forth from one Self to another Self at will. One may never get to know all of one's Selves; unknown Selves may wake up and come forth while the Waking Self of the moment is asleep. That's why some dreams we have can be so deeply disturbing: some Self we weren't aware of, and would find utterly repellent if we were aware of it, has woken up and taken control of our body's semi-consciousness in sleep.

Quite recently, reading Fritz Leiber, I discovered that he had much the same idea that each normal person containing a multitude of Selves, so I started looking around the web for possible sources he may have relied on. And I discovered one such source, a book by a social psychologist named Joseph Alexander Leighton, "Man and the Cosmos" (1922). I'm still looking through it, but the idea of multiple selves in every normal person is quite clearly presented.

Before that, the only other source I had been aware of was a series of books written by Charles Godfrey Leland (the author and editor of the famous "Aradia") late in his life: "The Mystic Will" (1899, originally titled "Have You a Strong Will"), "Flaxius: Leaves from the Life of an Immortal" (1903), and "The Alternate Sex; or, The Female Intellect in Man and the Masculine in Woman" (1904, posthumously published).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm! I wonder if Carl Jung might have encountered Leland's last book; it's very reminiscent of his concept of the anima and animus, the countersexual archetype.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's what I wondered, too, when I read Leland's "The Alternate Sex." Jung is likely to have read at least some of Leland's books, given his extensive reading on folklore and on magic, as well as his knowledge of English and his numerous English analyzands.

Jung's own very large library has been preserved as it was at the time of his death. There is even a published catalogue of the entire library, which I have never been able to see: "C. G. Jung-Bibliothek: Katalog" (Zürich-Küsnacht: privately printed, 1967; 117 pp.).

There doesn't appear to be even a single copy of this catalog in any academic or public library from which I might borrow it, so as to check whether it has any of Leland's books. Do any of the commentators here have access to a copy? If so, could you check it for Leland titles? There are likely to be copies in some of the better libraries owned by Jung Institutes in major US cities.
